title: The Forerunner Promised
subtitle: Gabriel at the altar of incense, and the word "Fear not, Zacharias"

stillness: Be still, and let the smoke of incense rise.
The multitude prays outside; you are within.
Let your prayer ascend as devotion, silent and unseen.
gospel: There was, in the days of Herod, king of Judea,
a certain priest named Zacharias, of the course of Abia;
and his wife was of the daughters of Aaron, named Elizabeth.
They were both just before God,
walking in all the commandments without blame.
And they had no son, for Elizabeth was barren,
and both were now advanced in years.
It came to pass, when he executed the priestly office,
that it fell to him by lot to offer incense,
going into the temple of the Lord.
And all the multitude of the people was praying outside,
at the hour of the incense.
And there appeared to him an angel of the Lord,
standing on the right side of the altar of incense.
And Zacharias was troubled, seeing him,
But the angel said to him:
Fear not, Zacharias, for your prayer is heard.
behold: See Zacharias, the priest, who is called 'mindful of the Lord':
a good prelate, who must carry the memory of God in his breast,
for his own salvation and the salvation of those under him.
He enters the temple by diligent care for the worship of God,
and he offers incense, which is devout prayer;
and the people pray through his guidance.
See him come out from the house where he is robed in the priestly vestments,
entering the temple of the Lord to set down the incense,
on the tenth day of the seventh month.
And the whole multitude of the people stands praying outside,
for it was not lawful for the people to enter the temple, but only the court.
See the angel of the Lord, Gabriel, standing on the right of the altar of incense,
which is also called the golden altar.
And Zacharias is troubled at the sight of a nature higher and more powerful than his own,
consider: By this the conception of the Forerunner is shown to be miraculous,
for it was not by nature alone, but by nature aided by divine grace.
The good is discerned from the wicked here:
the wicked man prolongs his horror,
which, as Bede says, is conquered by no better means than fearless faith;
but the good man is at once strengthened and consoled.
So the angel, strengthening him, says: Fear not, Zacharias.
As if he said: I come for your consolation.
For he was not praying for offspring, of which he no longer had hope,
distrusting because of his own age and his wife's,
so much that he would not believe the angel who promised it;
but he prayed for the sins of the people, and their redemption,
and the coming of the Messiah.
And because the salvation of the people was to come through Christ,
the angel announced to him a son to be born,
who, preaching penance and faith, would prepare the people for the Saviour.
He named the child also: he was to be called John,
which is interpreted, 'in whom is grace';
for he made manifest Christ, through whom grace and truth came to be.
Bede says: this name declares grace given first to his parents,
to whom, decrepit with age, a son would be born;
then to John himself, great before the Lord,
and even in his mother's womb to be enriched by the gift of the Holy Spirit;
and last to the sons of Israel, whom he would turn to their Lord God.
Solemn is the joy at the birth and begetting of the just, says Ambrose,
for the holy one is not only a grace to his parents, but the salvation of many.
We are admonished here to rejoice in the begetting of the saints.
prayer: Fear not, Zacharias, for your prayer is heard.
Lord, You did not despise the prayer of Your servant,
who prayed not for offspring he had ceased to hope for,
but for the sins of the people, and their redemption,
and the coming of the Messiah.
Make me also mindful of You,
to carry the memory of You in my breast,
for my own salvation and the salvation of others.
Let me enter Your temple by diligent care for Your worship,
and offer the incense of devout prayer.
And as You sent the Forerunner to prepare Your people,
so prepare my heart for the Saviour,
through whom grace and truth came to be.

latinJewel.latin: Joannes enim interpretatur, in quo est gratia; ipse vero Christum manifestavit, per quem gratia et veritas facta est.
latinJewel.english: For John is interpreted 'in whom is grace'; and he himself made Christ manifest, through whom grace and truth came to be.
